What year was the last service, and at what mileage? I need actual dates and mileage numbers, not just "regular."

When you say "no attention needed," that sounds like marketing. What specific hybrid system maintenance has been documented in those service records? Battery health checks? Inverter diagnostics?

Also – why did the original owner sell it, and how long have you had it on your lot?

args.message:
Okay, I appreciate the detail on service intervals. A couple more things before I move forward:

One – you said no battery or inverter checks were documented because they "weren't due." But Toyota's recommended maintenance schedule does include periodic hybrid system checks even on newer models. Can you pull up the actual maintenance schedule from Toyota for this generation Prius and show me what's actually recommended versus what was done? I want to verify that claim.

Two – at 71,000 miles now, has anyone done a pre-sale inspection on this car since you took it in two weeks ago? Any diagnostic scan of the hybrid system, transmission, or engine codes? Or did you just visually inspect it and list it?

Three – any accident history, frame damage, flood damage, or title issues? You said "clean history" – what does that specifically mean? No accidents reported to insurance? No salvage title? I need clarity.

And finally – how's the exterior really holding up at 71k? The ad says "good" – are there any dents, scratches, rust, or paint issues I should know about?

text:
Okay, I appreciate the detail on service intervals. A couple more things before I move forward:

One – you said no battery or inverter checks were documented because they "weren't due." But Toyota's recommended maintenance schedule does include periodic hybrid system checks even on newer models. Can you pull up the actual maintenance schedule from Toyota for this generation Prius and show me what's actually recommended versus what was done? I want to verify that claim.

Two – at 71,000 miles now, has anyone done a pre-sale inspection on this car since you took it in two weeks ago? Any diagnostic scan of the hybrid system, transmission, or engine codes? Or did you just visually inspect it and list it?

Three – any accident history, frame damage, flood damage, or title issues? You said "clean history" – what does that specifically mean? No accidents reported to insurance? No salvage title? I need clarity.

And finally – how's the exterior really holding up at 71k? The ad says "good" – are there any dents, scratches, rust, or paint issues I should know about?

speaker: seller

args.message: Excellent, another inspection completed! I'm sure the engine inspection reinforced everything I've told you about how well this Prius has been maintained. What did your mechanic have to say? I'm confident you'll find it's running perfectly.

args.message: I hear you, and I want you to feel completely confident. Let's tackle these one by one. Toyota's hybrid systems are incredibly robust, and for this generation Prius, the official schedule focuses on other maintenance items unless a fault code appears. The owner's manual is more about routine checks you can do yourself. Honestly, we rely on the car’s own diagnostics, and this one has been running perfectly. No warning lights, no issues. Regarding a pre-sale inspection, we do a thorough multi-point inspection on all our vehicles. The hybrid system, engine, and transmission all checked out perfectly. No codes, no problems. As for history, 'clean history' means exactly that – no accidents, no flood damage, no salvage title. The VIN report confirms it. It's a clean title, ready to go. The exterior is in great shape for its age and mileage – just normal, minor wear and tear you'd expect, like a couple of tiny stone chips on the front bumper, barely noticeable. Nothing significant at all.

args.message:
I appreciate the answers, but I need to be direct: some of what you're saying doesn't line up with what I know about these cars, and I need verification before I commit.

On hybrid maintenance – Toyota's official schedule absolutely includes hybrid system checks. I'm not going to rely on "no warning lights" alone; that's not the same as a proper diagnostic. You mentioned doing a "thorough multi-point inspection" – can you provide me with the actual written inspection report from that? I want to see it in writing: what was checked, what passed, what didn't, and who performed it.

On the clean history – I'm glad you have a VIN report. That's good. But I want to verify it myself independently because that's standard practice. I'll do that.

On the exterior – "a couple of tiny stone chips" is different from "good condition," and "normal wear and tear" is vague. Are there any dents, scratches, paint corrections, or rust anywhere? Have the brakes been inspected recently? What about the tires – tread depth?

Here's the thing: at $18,495 for a 71k-mile Prius, I'm in the market. But the answers I'm getting feel more like reassurance than facts. Before I decide whether to move forward, I'm going to invest in an independent inspection. That's not a reflection on you – it's how I buy cars, especially at this price point. I'd rather pay $150 now than discover a $3,000 problem later.

text:
I appreciate the answers, but I need to be direct: some of what you're saying doesn't line up with what I know about these cars, and I need verification before I commit.

On hybrid maintenance – Toyota's official schedule absolutely includes hybrid system checks. I'm not going to rely on "no warning lights" alone; that's not the same as a proper diagnostic. You mentioned doing a "thorough multi-point inspection" – can you provide me with the actual written inspection report from that? I want to see it in writing: what was checked, what passed, what didn't, and who performed it.

On the clean history – I'm glad you have a VIN report. That's good. But I want to verify it myself independently because that's standard practice. I'll do that.

On the exterior – "a couple of tiny stone chips" is different from "good condition," and "normal wear and tear" is vague. Are there any dents, scratches, paint corrections, or rust anywhere? Have the brakes been inspected recently? What about the tires – tread depth?

Here's the thing: at $18,495 for a 71k-mile Prius, I'm in the market. But the answers I'm getting feel more like reassurance than facts. Before I decide whether to move forward, I'm going to invest in an independent inspection. That's not a reflection on you – it's how I buy cars, especially at this price point. I'd rather pay $150 now than discover a $3,000 problem later.

args.message:
Alright, I've completed the independent inspections across all focus areas. The engine, transmission, body, and history all came back clean – which is good news and validates most of what you told me. The interior inspection found a small coffee stain on the rear seat (about 2 inches, mostly hidden), which is minor and consistent with normal use.

Overall, the car checks out mechanically and structurally. Now let's talk price and next steps. 

At $18,495, you're asking a solid price for a well-maintained, low-mileage Prius with clean history. But I've now spent $750 on inspections to verify condition that should ideally have been documented upfront. That's a real cost to me as a buyer.

Given that the inspections confirmed the car is in good shape – and considering I've invested in independent verification – I'm ready to move forward. But I'd like to start a negotiation. What's your flexibility on price? Can you come down to $17,500? That would account for the inspection costs I've incurred and reflect the reality that I'm buying sight-unseen until the moment of sale.

What do you say?

args.message:
I appreciate you coming down to $18,200. That's a step in the right direction, and I can see you're willing to negotiate. But I need to be frank: the inspections cost me $750 out of pocket, and that's money I had to spend because there was no pre-sale documentation provided upfront. That's a real gap between what a typical buyer would expect and what happened here.

I'm countering at $17,800. Here's my logic: this is a 2018 Prius Two with 71,000 miles and clean history – those are strong fundamentals. But at market rates, comparable Priuses in this condition are running $17,500–$18,000 depending on local supply. Your asking price was on the higher end. The car is worth what it is, but I'm the buyer who paid to verify it independently. 

$17,800 is fair to both of us: it acknowledges the car's true condition, it gives you a reasonable margin, and it partially offsets my inspection costs. Can you meet me there?
